reasoning: The kernel uses 255 registers per thread (the hardware maximum), which is the smoking gun for register spilling. The kernel declares 576 floats of local arrays (STATE0+STATE1+TAPS+MIX = 192+192+128+64), far exceeding the register file capacity, forcing massive spills to local memory (which resides in DRAM). The 90.3% long scoreboard stalls reflect threads waiting on local memory load-back latencies from spilled registers, while the 109.8% DRAM utilization and L2 hit rate of 100% (L1 misses going to L2, with spill traffic also hitting DRAM) confirm heavy local memory traffic. The extremely low arithmetic intensity (0.05 FLOP/byte) results from the enormous volume of spill/fill traffic rather than useful global memory accesses, and `__launch_bounds__(128, 1)` with 255 registers limits occupancy to 15.9%, compounding the latency-hiding problem.

The 90.3% long scoreboard stalls reflect threads waiting on local memory load-back latencies from spilled registers, while the 109.8% DRAM utilization and L2 hit rate of 100% (L1 misses going to L2, with spill traffic also hitting DRAM) confirm heavy local memory traffic. The extremely low arithmetic intensity (0.05 FLOP/byte) results from the enormous volume of spill/fill traffic rather than useful global memory accesses, and `__launch_bounds__(128, 1)` with 255 registers limits occupancy to 15.9%, compounding the latency-hiding problem.
